elements.sent-2411-001: Given a connected graph $G=(V,E)$ and a crossing family $\mathcal{C}$ over ground set $V$ such that $|\delta_G(U)|\geq 2$ for every $U\in \mathcal{C}$, we prove there exists a strong orientation of $G$ for $\mathcal{C}$, i.e., an orientation of $G$ such that each set in $\mathcal{C}$ has at least one outgoing and at least one incoming arc.
elements.sent-2411-002: This implies the main conjecture in Chudnovsky et al.\ (Disjoint dijoins.
elements.sent-2411-003: Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B, 120:18--35, 2016).
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, in every minimal counterexample to the Edmonds-Giles conjecture where the minimum weight of a dicut is $2$, the arcs of nonzero weight must be disconnected.
elements.sent-2411-005: A set collection $\mathcal{C}\subseteq 2^V$ is called a crossing family over the ground set $V$ if $U\cap W,U\cup W\in \mathcal{C}$ for all sets $U,W\in\mathcal{C}$ that cross, i.e., $U\cap W\neq \emptyset$ and $U\cup W\neq V$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Given a graph $G=(V,E)$ and a crossing family $\mathcal{C}$ over ground set $V$, a strong orientation of $G$ for $\mathcal{C$} is an orientation $\overrightarrow{G}$ of the edges of $G$ such that $\delta^+_{\overrightarrow{G (U), \delta^-_{\overrightarrow{G (U) \neq \emptyset$ for every $U\in \mathcal{C}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: In this note, we prove the following theorem by using tools from integer programming and combinatorial optimization.
elements.sent-2411-008: Let $G=(V,E)$ be a connected graph, and let $\mathcal{C}$ be a crossing family over ground set $V$ such that $|\delta_G(U)|\geq 2$ for all $U\in \mathcal{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Then there exists a strong orientation of $G$ for $\mathcal{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-010: The connectivity of $G$ is crucial for this theorem, and without it the theorem would not hold.
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, let $G=(V,E)$ be the graph displayed in \cref{fig:example_disconnected whose edges correspond to the solid lines, and let $\mathcal{C}$ be the family of all nonempty proper vertex subsets with no incoming dashed arc.}
elements.sent-2411-012: In that conjecture, $G$ is a tree, and $\mathcal{C =\{\emptyset\neq U\subsetneq V: \delta_D^-(U)=\emptyset\}$ for some digraph $D$ over vertex set $V$ (such a family is always crossing).}
elements.sent-2411-013: Our motivation for studying this problem stems from Woodall's conjecture~\cite{Woodall and Edmonds and Giles' attempt~\cite{EDMONDS1977185 to extend this to the weighted setting.. Let us elaborate.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: A weighted digraph is a pair $(D=(V,A),w)$, where $D$ is a digraph and $w\in \mathbb{Z ^A_{\ge 0}$.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Given a digraph $D=(V,A)$ and $U\subseteq V$, we define $\delta_D^+(U)\coloneqq \{(u,v)\in A\colon u\in U, v\in V\setminus U\}$ to be the set of outgoing arcs of $U$, and we let $\delta_D^-(U)\coloneqq \{(v,u)\in A\colon u\in U, v\in V\setminus U\}$ denote the set of incoming arcs of $U$.
elements.sent-2411-017: If $D$ is clear from the context, we might omit the subscript $D$ and just write $\delta^+(U)$, $\delta^-(U)$ and $\delta(U)$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-018: For a vertex $v$, we write $\delta^{+/- (v)\coloneqq \delta^{+/- (\{v\})$ and $\delta(v)\coloneqq \delta(\{v\})$.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: A dicut in $D$ is a directed cut of the form $C\coloneqq \delta^+(U)$, where $\emptyset\neq U\subsetneq V$ such that $\delta^-(U)=\emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-020: A subset $J\subseteq A$ is a dijoin if its contraction renders $G$ strongly connected.
elements.sent-2411-021: Equivalently, $J$ is a dijoin if and only if $J$ intersects every dicut.
elements.sent-2411-022: The latter characterization implies that the maximum number $\nu$ of pairwise disjoint dijoins in $D$ is upper bounded by the minimum size $\tau$ of a dicut.
elements.sent-2411-024: While it is known that if $\tau\geq 2$ then $\nu\geq 2$, Woodall's conjecture remains wide open in general, even if $\tau=3$ and the digraph is planar.
elements.sent-2411-025: Abdi, Cornu{\'e jols and Zlatin~\cite{Abdi2023 have shown that the arcs of $D$ can be partitioned into a dijoin and a $(\tau-1)$-dijoin, which by definition intersects every dicut at least $\tau-1$ times; see~\cite{Abdi2024 for a short proof.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Recently, by extending an idea of Shepherd and Vetta~\cite{Shepherd05 , Cornu{\'e jols, Liu and Ravi~\cite{10.1007/978-3-031-59835-7_6 have shown that $\nu\geq \tau/6$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Edmonds and Giles~\cite{EDMONDS1977185 proposed a weighted version of Woodall's conjecture: Given a weighted digraph $(D=(V,A),w)$ and $\tau_w\in\mathbb{Z _{\geq 0}$ such that the minimum weight of a dicut is at least $\tau_w$, do there exist dijoins $J_1,\dots,J_{\tau_w}$ such that for every arc $a\in A$, $|\{i\in[\tau_w]: a\in J_i\}|\leq w(a)$? Note that by introducing parallel arcs for arcs of nonzero weight, it suffices to consider weight vectors $w\in\{0,1\}^A$; hence, we will assume that all weights are either $0$ or $1$ in the sequel.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: The Edmonds-Giles conjecture was refuted by Schrijver~\cite{SCHRIJVER1980213 , providing a counterexample for $\tau_w = 2$ (see \cref{fig:Schrijvers_example ).}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this example, the weight-$1$ arcs form three distinct weakly connected components.
elements.sent-2411-030: The main conjecture in~\cite{CHUDNOVSKY201618 (Conjecture 1.4) is that the Edmonds-Giles conjecture is true for $\tau_w=2$ if the weight-$1$ arcs form a spanning weakly connected subdigraph.}
elements.sent-2411-031: They proved this conjecture in two special cases: when $D$ is planar, and when the weight-$1$ arcs form a subdivision of a caterpillar.
elements.sent-2411-032: The proofs of these two cases, which are quite different and purely graph theoretic in nature, are technical and sensitive to extension.
elements.sent-2411-033: For example, even in the case when $D$ is planar, it was unknown prior to this work whether one could drop the `spanning' condition on the subdigraph of the weight-$1$ arcs (see step 2 in \cref{sec:proof-2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-034: In this note, we verify the conjecture of~\cite{CHUDNOVSKY201618 as a consequence of \cref{thm:strong_orientation .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: A bridge in a graph is an edge whose deletion increases the number of connected components.
elements.sent-2411-036: A graph is bridge-connected if all its bridges, if any, belong to the same connected component.
elements.sent-2411-037: In particular, adding an isolated vertex or a disjoint $2$-edge-connected graph does not affect bridge-connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-038: A digraph is weakly bridge-connected if its underlying undirected graph is bridge-connected.
elements.sent-2411-040: Let $(D=(V,A),w)$ be a weighted digraph with $w\in \{0,1\}^A$, where the weight of every dicut is at least $2$.
elements.sent-2411-041: If the weight-$1$ arcs form a weakly bridge-connected subdigraph, then there exist two disjoint dijoins of $D$ contained in the weight-$1$ arcs.
elements.sent-2411-042: This theorem extends a result of~\cite{Shepherd05 (Theorem 1.10), which proves the above in the case when every connected component of the underlying undirected graph of the weight-$1$ arcs is $2$-edge-connected.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: In \cref{sec:prelim , we recap some basic notions and known results from combinatorial optimization and the theory of linear and integer programming that we will rely on to establish the two theorems.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The proofs of \cref{thm:strong_orientation and \cref{thm:tree_orientation are given in \cref{sec:proof-1 and \cref{sec:proof-2 , respectively.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-046: Let $D=(V,A)$ be a digraph and let $b\in \mathbb{R ^V$ with $b(V)=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: A $b$-transshipment is a vector $y\in \mathbb{R ^A$ with $y(\delta^+(v))-y(\delta^-(v))=b_v$ for all $v\in V$.}
elements.sent-2411-048: There is a classical result providing a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of a $b$-transshipment obeying given upper and lower bounds.
elements.sent-2411-049: Then there exists a $b$-transshipment $y$ with $\ell\leq y\leq u$ if and only if \begin{equation}b(U)\leq u(\delta^+(U))-\ell(\delta^-(U)) \quad\forall U\subseteq V. \label{eq:condition_transshipment}\end{equation} Moreover, if $b,\ell,u$ are integral, then $y$ can be chosen to be integral.
elements.sent-2411-050: By setting $\ell\coloneqq-\infty$ and $u\coloneqq +\infty$, it follows from \cref{b-transshipment that there exists a $b$-transshipment if and only if $b(U)=0$ for every $U\subseteq V$ with $\delta(U)=\emptyset$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In particular, if $D$ is weakly connected, there always exists a $b$-transshipment.
elements.sent-2411-052: Given a crossing family $\mathcal{C}$, we say that a set function $f\colon \mathcal{C}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ is crossing-submodular if \begin{equation}f(U\cap W)+f(U\cup W)\leq f(U)+f(W) \quad \forall U,W\in \mathcal{C}\text{ that cross.} \label{eq:def_crossing_submodular}\end{equation} Note that the class of crossing-submodular functions over a fixed family $\mathcal{C}$ is closed under addition.

elements.sent-2411-058: The system $Ax\leq b$ of linear inequalities is called totally dual integral (TDI) if for every cost vector $c\in \mathbb{Z ^n$ for which $\max\{c^\top x\colon Ax\leq b\}$ has an optimum solution, its dual $\min\{ b^\top y\colon A^\top y = c, y\geq 0\}$ has an integral optimum solution.}
elements.sent-2411-059: If the right-hand side $b$ is integral and the system $Ax\leq b$ is TDI, then the polyhedron $P\coloneqq \{x\colon Ax\leq b\}$ is integral~\cite{EDMONDS1977185 .}
elements.sent-2411-060: A system $Ax\leq b$ is called box-TDI if for every pair of integral vectors $\ell\leq u$, the system $Ax\leq b, \ell\leq x\leq u$ is TDI.
elements.sent-2411-061: In particular, every box-TDI system is also TDI.
elements.sent-2411-062: An example for a box-TDI system is the intersection of two `base systems', as stated formally below.

elements.sent-2411-090: Suppose the set $A_1$ of the weight-$1$ arcs forms a weakly bridge-connected subdigraph.
elements.sent-2411-091: Our goal is to find two disjoint dijoins of $D$ contained in $A_1$.
elements.sent-2411-092: We proceed by induction on $|V|$, and in three steps.
elements.sent-2411-093: The first two steps are routine, and the third step uses \cref{thm:strong_orientation to prove the base case.}
elements.sent-2411-095: We then contract $C$ in $(D,w)$ to obtain another weighted digraph $(D,w)/C$; the weight of an arc that is not contracted remains the same.
elements.sent-2411-096: Clearly, the minimum weight of a dicut of $(D,w)/C$ remains at least $2$, so by the induction hypothesis, there exist two disjoint dijoins $J_1,J_2$ of $D/C$ contained in the weight-$1$ arcs of $(D,w)/C$.
elements.sent-2411-097: We then uncontract $C$, traverse the cycle in some order, denote by $J'_1\subseteq C$ the set of arcs that agree with this order, and denote $J'_2\coloneqq C\setminus J'_1$.
elements.sent-2411-098: It can be readily checked that $J_1\cup J'_1,J_2\cup J'_2$ are disjoint dijoins of $D$ contained in $A_1$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Suppose $V$ contains a node $v_0$ that is not incident to any weight-$1$ arc.
elements.sent-2411-100: We then delete $v_0$, and add a weight-$0$ arc from every in-neighbor of $v_0$ to every out-neighbor of $v_0$.\footnote{Note that this reduction does not necessarily preserve planarity, and so cannot be applied to drop the `spanning' condition in (\cite{CHUDNOVSKY201618 , Theorem 2.2).}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Denote by $(D',w')$ the new weighted digraph.
elements.sent-2411-102: It can be readily checked that the minimum weight of a dicut in $(D',w')$ is at least $2$, so by the induction hypothesis, we find two disjoint dijoins $J_1,J_2$ of $D'$ contained in the weight-$1$ arcs of $(D',w')$.
elements.sent-2411-103: It can be readily checked that $J_1,J_2$ are also disjoint dijoins of $D$ contained in the weight-$1$ arcs of $(D,w)$.
elements.sent-2411-104: We arrive at the base case where $A_1$ contains no cycle, and the weight-$1$ arcs span every vertex of $D$.
elements.sent-2411-105: As $A_1$ forms a weakly bridge-connected subdigraph, it follows that the underlying undirected graph of $D_1\coloneqq(V,A_1)$ is a (spanning) tree.
elements.sent-2411-107: Since every dicut of $(D,w)$ has weight at least $2$, it follows that $|\delta_{D_1 (U)|\geq 2$ for all $U\in \mathcal{C}$.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Thus, by \cref{thm:strong_orientation , there exists a subset $J\subseteq A_1$ such that every set in $\mathcal{C}$ has at least one outgoing and at least one incoming arc in $(A_1\setminus J)\cup J^{-1}$, where $J^{-1}$ is obtained from $J$ after flipping each arc in the set.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Subsequently, $J$ and $A_1\setminus J$ are disjoint dijoins of $D$.\qed.
elements.section-2411-005: The case of two connected components \label{sec:2_components}
elements.sent-2411-110: Given that in the counterexample to the Edmonds-Giles conjecture displayed in \cref{fig:Schrijvers_example , as well as all other known counterexamples~\cite{Cornuejols02,Williams04 , the weight-$1$ arcs form at least three weakly connected components, it appears natural to ask whether \cref{thm:tree_orientation extends to the case where the weight-$1$ arcs have at most two weakly bridge-connected components.}}}

elements.section-2411-006: Strengthening sets for a crossing family
elements.sent-2411-118: Let $D=(V,A)$ be a digraph, and let $\mathcal{C}$ be a crossing family over ground set $V$.
elements.sent-2411-119: A strengthening set of $D$ for $\mathcal{C$} is a subset $J\subseteq A$ such that every set in $\mathcal{C}$ has at least one outgoing arc after the arcs in $J$ are flipped.
elements.sent-2411-121: Let $\tau\geq 2$ be an integer, let $D=(V,A)$ be a weakly connected digraph, and let $\mathcal{C}$ be a crossing family such that $|\delta_D^-(U)|+(\tau-1)|\delta_D^+(U)|\geq \tau$ for all $U\in \mathcal{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-122: Then $A$ can be partitioned into $\tau$ strengthening sets of $D$ for $\mathcal{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-123: Observe that the inequality equivalently asks that $|\delta_D(U)|\geq 2$ for all $U\in \mathcal{C}$, and $|\delta^-_D(U)|\geq \tau$ for all $U\in \mathcal{C}$ such that $\delta^+_D(U)=\emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Both of these inequalities are necessary for $A$ to be partitioned into $\tau$ strengthening sets of $D$ for $\mathcal{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-125: It is equivalent to Woodall's conjecture if $\mathcal{C =\{\emptyset\neq U\subsetneq V : \delta_D^+(U)=\emptyset\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-126: It becomes (\cite{CHUDNOVSKY201618 , Conjecture 1.5) in the case where $\mathcal{C =\{\emptyset\neq U\subsetneq V : \delta_{D\cup D' ^+(U)=\emptyset\}$ for another digraph $D'$ over vertex set $V$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Finally, when $\mathcal{C =\{U\subsetneq V:U\neq \emptyset\}$, we obtain the following conjecture that appears in an unpublished note by Schrijver~\cite{Schrijver-note .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Given a digraph $D=(V,A)$, a strengthening set is a subset $J\subseteq A$ such that $(D\setminus J)\cup J^{-1}$ is strongly connected.
elements.sent-2411-129: Then $A$ can be partitioned into $\tau$ strengthening sets.
elements.sent-2411-130: It can be readily checked that if $A$ is partitioned into $\tau$ strengthening sets, then it has $\tau$ disjoint dijoins.
elements.sent-2411-131: Conversely, there is a digraph $D'$ such that every packing of $\tau$ dijoins of $D'$ yields a partition of $A$ into $\tau$ strengthening sets of $D$~\cite{Schrijver-note .\footnote{To obtain $D'$, simply replace every arc $a=(u,v)$ of $D$ with $(u,t_a)$ and $(\tau-1)$ parallel copies of $(v,t_a)$ for a new node $t_a$.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: In summary, for a specific digraph, the conjecture above is at least as strong as Woodall's conjecture, while for all digraphs, the two conjectures are equivalent.
